title: research: assess mise en place article for academic conversion
data: extract bead closure timestamps from hackathon
description:
## Context
The paper's implementation timeline currently relies on git commits, but the first commit is monolithic (76 files, 13,143 lines). This makes the argument about implementation speed look weak. Bead closure timestamps provide a much richer timeline with discrete data points showing how work progressed during the implementation phase.

## Task
Read the hackathon project's bead data at `~/hackathon-infactory/.beads/issues.jsonl` (READ-ONLY — do not modify anything in that repo). Extract:

1. All bead records with their:
   - ID
   - Title
   - Priority
   - Status (open/closed)
   - Created timestamp
   - Closed timestamp (if closed)
   - Type (task/bug)

2. Compute:
   - Implementation timeline: when were beads created and closed?
   - Closure rate over time (beads closed per 10-minute window)
   - Distribution of closure times (min, max, median, mean, stddev)
   - How many beads were closed before the first git commit (22:24 UTC)?
   - Bug beads vs feature beads: closure patterns

3. Save results to `~/cfp/mise/data/bead-timestamps.md` with:
   - Summary statistics
   - Chronological timeline of bead closures
   - Any patterns that support the "parallel agent execution" narrative

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All bead records extracted with timestamps
- [ ] Chronological closure timeline computed
- [ ] Statistics saved to data/bead-timestamps.md
- [ ] No modifications to hackathon repo

title: assembly: merge sections, references, figures, and verify page count

description:

Paper uses ACM Primary Article Template for VibeX 2026 workshop (co-located with EASE 2026).

## Task
Set up paper/ directory with acmart template. Create main.tex with full section scaffolding matching the plan (6 sections + references). Include placeholder content indicating what goes in each section. Set up references.bib with known citations. Create figures/ directory.

## Acceptance Criteria

title: paper: fix conclusion ending and one-shot claim
description:
## Context
The paper's conclusion ends with "The code, increasingly, is the easy part. The hard part---knowing what to build and why---remains a distinctly human contribution." This mirrors the Substack article's ending and reads as a cute sign-off rather than academic writing. Also, context-fluency.tex contains "produced the context that made the Socratic tutor correct on first implementation" which sounds like bragging.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/sections/research-agenda.tex` and `paper/sections/context-fluency.tex`. DO NOT touch any other files.

### In research-agenda.tex (conclusion subsection):
1. **Remove the cute ending**: Delete the last two sentences: "The code, increasingly, is the easy part. The hard part---knowing what to build and why---remains a distinctly human contribution."
2. **Replace with academic closing**: End with something that reinforces the invitation to further research and the positioning of this work as a starting framework. Something like: "We invite empirical validation, replication across domains, and the development of tools that support preparation-phase work as the field moves from ad-hoc agent interaction toward disciplined context engineering." (Adapt this — the key is it should end on a research-forward note, not a pithy aphorism.)

### In context-fluency.tex (domain encoding item):
3. **Remove "one-shot" bragging**: The sentence "Twenty minutes of dictated pedagogical intuitions produced the context that made the Socratic tutor correct on first implementation" implies one-shot perfection. Rewrite to be factual without the bragging tone: something like "Twenty minutes of dictated pedagogical intuitions produced context that substantially reduced iteration on the domain-specific tutor component." The point is the same (dictation was effective) without claiming perfection.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cute sign-off ending removed from conclusion
- [ ] Academic research-forward ending added
- [ ] "Correct on first implementation" claim softened
- [ ] Paper compiles cleanly
- [ ] No new overfull hbox warnings

title: harden: sharpen context fluency and research agenda
description:
## Context
Reviewers noted that context fluency is introduced as a concept but never clearly distinguished from the methodology phases, and that it lacks operationalization. The research agenda is solid but could be tightened.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/sections/context-fluency.tex` and `paper/sections/research-agenda.tex`. DO NOT touch any other .tex files or references.bib.

### In context-fluency.tex:
1. **Distinguish fluency from methodology**: Add 1-2 sentences at the start of the section (after the opening paragraph) clarifying the relationship: "Where the methodology (Section 3) describes a process—sequential phases producing defined artifacts—context fluency describes the practitioner skill that makes the process effective. The distinction is analogous to that between a recipe (process) and culinary judgment (skill): the recipe structures the work, but the outcome depends on the cook's ability to execute it."
2. **Connect to knowledge management**: Add a brief reference connecting the "Domain encoding" component to Nonaka & Takeuchi's SECI model (externalization mode: tacit-to-explicit). Use \cite{nonaka1995knowledge}. This ties context fluency to established theory and answers the "thin theoretical grounding" critique.
3. **Tighten pedagogical scaffolding paragraph**: The paragraph comparing context fluency to lesson planning is good but slightly long. Trim by 1-2 sentences if possible without losing the core insight.

### In research-agenda.tex:
4. **Tighten tractability note**: The sentence "We believe the most immediately tractable is RQ1" is fine but could be stronger. Change to: "RQ1 is the most immediately tractable, addressable with paired tasks and a modest participant pool."
5. **Strengthen conclusion framing**: In the conclusion subsection, where we say "a formalization of practices that experienced practitioners already converge on independently"—this is good and should stay. But add one sentence connecting back to the SECI model: "The methodology formalizes what Nonaka and Takeuchi~\cite{nonaka1995knowledge} term externalization—converting tacit practitioner knowledge into explicit artifacts that agents can consume."

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clear distinction between methodology (process) and context fluency (skill) added
- [ ] Domain encoding connected to SECI externalization mode
- [ ] Pedagogical scaffolding paragraph trimmed
- [ ] RQ1 tractability note tightened (remove "We believe")
- [ ] Conclusion references SECI model for theoretical grounding
- [ ] Paper still compiles cleanly with `latexmk -pdf main.tex`
- [ ] No new overfull hbox warnings introduced

title: harden: fix unsourced rework claim and citation format
description:
## Context
Critic cycle 2 flagged two issues: (1) the phrase "below practitioner-reported norms" in the rework ratio sentence has no citation, and (2) references.bib uses "Available at \url{...}" in the note field instead of proper url fields.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/sections/case-study.tex` and `paper/references.bib`. DO NOT touch any other .tex files.

### In case-study.tex:
1. **Remove unsourced comparison**: Find the sentence about rework ratio that says "below practitioner-reported norms for unstructured agentic workflows." Remove just the comparison phrase "below practitioner-reported norms for unstructured agentic workflows" — there is no citable source for this claim. The sentence should end after reporting the ratio itself. Keep the rework ratio data (33%, 3 of 9 commits).

### In references.bib:
2. **Move URLs from note to url field**: For every entry that has `note = {Available at \url{...}}`, extract the URL into a proper `url = {}` field and remove the "Available at" note. Keep any non-URL content in the note field (e.g., Karpathy's "Term entered Merriam-Webster..." note should stay, but the URL moves to url field). Keep Veracode's note content (the 45% statistic) but move any URL to url field.
3. **Add DOIs for arXiv papers**: For the 4 arXiv entries (dakhel2025humanai, alomar2025promptware, peng2023copilot, developer2023mental), add a `doi` field using the arXiv DOI format: `doi = {10.48550/arXiv.XXXX.XXXXX}` where the number matches the arXiv ID already in the entry. Keep the url field too.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "practitioner-reported norms" comparison removed from case-study.tex
- [ ] All "Available at \url{...}" moved from note to url field in references.bib
- [ ] arXiv DOIs added for all 4 arXiv entries
- [ ] Non-URL note content preserved (Karpathy dictionary note, Veracode stats, Gartner description)
- [ ] Paper compiles cleanly with `latexmk -pdf main.tex`
- [ ] Zero overfull hbox warnings

title: paper: revise to camera-ready (Changes 1-5,7-10)
spec: VibeX 2026 camera-ready revisions
description:
## 1. Overview

Revise the accepted VibeX 2026 paper "Mise en Place for Agentic Coding"
into its camera-ready form for ACM ICPS proceedings, addressing the
reviewer feedback (Reviewer 1 weak reject, Reviewers 2 & 3 weak accept).

**In scope:**
- Required revisions explicitly requested by reviewers (acronym, prompt
  engineering background, tables/figures, team variation, novelty
  framing)
- SIGSOFT Open Science Policy compliance (research artifact bundle +
  Artifact Availability section)
- Honest limitations rewrite + expanded future work
- Format port from current `[sigconf,review]` author/review version to
  ACM ICPS camera-ready
- Final PDF rebuild + verification of page limit

**Out of scope:**
- New empirical work, new case studies, new comparative data (per
  brief: "Do not attempt to address Review 1's empirical critique
  through new studies, new case data, or fabricated comparison points.")
- arXiv submission (separate downstream step the owner handles)
- Zenodo upload (we produce the bundle; owner uploads + edits the DOI in)
- EasyChair upload (owner does this after PR review + approval)

**Sources informing this spec:**
- Acceptance email (verbatim reviewer comments captured in this bead)
- Project CLAUDE.md
- Hackathon repo `~/hackathon-infactory/refs/competitor-ideas.md`
  (12-team pitch table — source for team variation observations)
- Existing paper at `paper/main.pdf` (5pp, sigconf format)
- Existing data files at `data/*.md`
- `REVIEW-NOTES.md` from initial submission

## 2. Current State / Baseline

- `paper/main.tex`: `\documentclass[sigconf,review]{acmart}`, single
  corresponding author already filled (Andrew Zigler / LinearB), 6
  section files in `paper/sections/`, 16 BibTeX entries, no figures
  directory populated, 1 TikZ inline figure (Figure 1 methodology
  diagram) and 1 inline table (Table 1 timeline) embedded in section
  files.
- 5 pages compiled via `latexmk -pdf main.tex`.
- "Mise en place" written out in full ~30+ times across the prose.
- No prompt engineering literature cited.
- No quantitative figures beyond the inline timeline table.
- Limitations subsection exists in research-agenda.tex but defends
  rather than acknowledges Reviewer 1's empirical critique head-on.
- No Artifact Availability section.
- `paper/figures/` directory exists but is empty.
- Data files at `data/`: `git-timeline.md`, `beads-metrics.md`,
  `bead-timestamps.md`, `session-analysis.md`. These cover the
  practitioner's own team's metrics; team-level variation across the
  other 11 teams is NOT yet extracted.

## 3. Changes and Decisions

### Change 1 — Introduce MEP acronym (Reviewer 2 must-do)
- **What:** On first occurrence of "mise en place" in the abstract OR
  introduction, introduce as "mise en place (MEP)". Use "MEP" for the
  rest of the paper for the methodology references; keep "mise en
  place" only for the metaphor framing where the culinary term itself
  is the point (≤3 occurrences).
- **Why:** Reviewer 2 explicitly requested.
- **How:** Find-and-replace pass after first introduction.

### Change 2 — Add prompt engineering background (Reviewer 2 must-do)
- **What:** 1–2 paragraphs in `related-work.tex` situating MEP against
  the prompt engineering literature. Cite at least 3 foundational /
  recent works (e.g., Brown et al. 2020 GPT-3 few-shot; Wei et al.
  2022 chain-of-thought; Liu et al. 2023 prompt engineering survey;
  Anthropic 2024 prompting guides). Distinguish MEP from prompt
  engineering: MEP is preparation methodology ABOUT the development
  context (specs, decompositions, externalized tacit knowledge);
  prompt engineering is instruction-tuning of individual model
  invocations. Both engineer context, but at different scopes.
- **Why:** Reviewer 2 noted gap.
- **How:** Insert after the existing context-engineering paragraph in
  related-work.tex; add 3 BibTeX entries to references.bib.

### Change 3 — Tables / figures presenting hackathon results (Reviewer 2 must-do)
- **What:** Three new visual artifacts:
  - **Table 2:** 12-team artifact summary (team idea, technical
    approach if known, observed agentic-tool use). Sourced from
    `~/hackathon-infactory/refs/competitor-ideas.md` and any session
    notes. Anonymize team-member names.
  - **Figure 2:** Per-bead completion time distribution (histogram or
    sorted bar chart from `data/bead-timestamps.md`). Show median
    5.9 min line.
  - **Figure 3 OR augmented Table 1:** Deployment timeline visualization
    (9 commits in 52 minutes from `data/git-timeline.md`) — TikZ
    timeline is most readable in tight space.
- **Why:** Reviewer 2 explicitly asked.
- **How:** TikZ for figures (consistent with existing Figure 1);
  booktabs for table. Keep visual budget tight — paper must stay ≤6
  pages.

### Change 4 — Team-level variation discussion (Reviewer 2 must-do)
- **What:** A short subsection (~150–200 words) in `case-study.tex`
  describing variation across the 12 hackathon teams: which teams
  used heavy task-decomposition vs. iterative prompting, which used
  single-agent vs. multi-agent workflows, what artifacts they
  produced. Acknowledge limited observational data (we did not
  instrument other teams) but offer the patterns we did observe.
  Frame as motivating future work (multi-team replication study).
- **Why:** Reviewer 2 specifically asked.
- **How:** Read the competitor-ideas.md table + any session-log
  references to other teams. Summarize qualitatively. Anonymize.

### Change 5 — Sharpen novelty framing (Reviewer 3 must-do)
- **What:** A clearly-marked paragraph (likely in related-work.tex or
  the intro) that names what is well-established (preparation, specs,
  task decomposition) and explicitly distinguishes MEP from:
  (a) traditional spec-driven development — MEP integrates
  externalized tacit knowledge, not just specs;
  (b) prompt engineering — MEP operates at the workflow scope,
  prompt engineering at the invocation scope;
  (c) iterative / exploratory AI coding workflows — MEP frontloads
  the alignment work that iterative workflows pay incrementally.
  Argue why agentic coding NEEDS this preparation in ways human-only
  workflows do not (agents lack the tacit context humans carry; they
  cannot iterate cheaply without rewrites).
- **Why:** Reviewer 3 noted these ideas are well-established.
- **How:** New paragraph or subsection. Cite the comparison
  literature.

### Change 6 — Research artifact bundle (Reviewer 2 must-do, SIGSOFT compliance)
- **What:** A new `artifact/` directory at repo root containing:
  - `README.md` describing the artifact, license (CC-BY 4.0 default),
    Zenodo upload instructions, and what's inside
  - `data/` — anonymized exports of the timing / commit / beads data
    (already mostly anonymized; verify no PII)
  - `prompts/` — the hackathon prompts and agent configurations used
    (extract from hackathon-infactory `.claude/plans/` and
    `CLAUDE.md`)
  - `agent-harness/` — any context-engineering scaffolds used
    (CLAUDE.md, beads schema, etc.)
  - `LICENSE` — CC-BY 4.0 for data, MIT for code
  - Add an "Artifact Availability" section to the paper pointing to
    a placeholder Zenodo URL `https://zenodo.org/[TBD]` that the
    owner replaces with the actual DOI after upload.
- **Why:** SIGSOFT Open Science Policy required for compliance.
- **How:** Mirror `data/`, copy hackathon-repo planning files (with
  permission per CLAUDE.md "READ-ONLY data source"), document.

### Change 7 — Honest limitations rewrite (should-do)
- **What:** Rewrite the limitations subsection to acknowledge head-on:
  (a) single hackathon, single practitioner-led case study;
  (b) no control group, no iteration-first comparison;
  (c) results may reflect operator expertise as much as methodology.
  Frame these as motivating future work, not as fatal flaws. Visibly
  address Reviewer 1's empirical concern.
- **Why:** Reviewer 1 weak-reject was driven by this. Honest framing
  shows the paper takes the critique seriously.
- **How:** Rewrite the existing Limitations subsection in
  research-agenda.tex (or move to its own subsection if cleaner).

### Change 8 — Expanded future work (should-do)
- **What:** Future work section adds three explicit research lines:
  (a) comparative study (preparation-first vs. iteration-first);
  (b) multi-team replication across diverse codebases;
  (c) longitudinal study of context fluency development.
  Convert Reviewer 1's critique into a research roadmap.
- **Why:** Reviewer 1's empirical concern + Reviewer 2's
  multi-codebase suggestion both fold into this.
- **How:** Extend the existing research-agenda.tex with these as
  named future work tracks (or fold into existing RQs if they map).

### Change 9 — ACM ICPS camera-ready format
- **What:** Drop `review` option from documentclass; verify acmart
  ICPS-compatible metadata (acmConference is set; CCS concepts
  present; copyright statement appropriate for workshop / ICPS).
  Verify de-anonymization is complete (single-blind was original,
  so author info already filled; double-check for stray
  "[Author Name]" placeholders).
- **Why:** Required for camera-ready submission.
- **How:** Edit `\documentclass[sigconf,review]{acmart}` →
  `\documentclass[sigconf]{acmart}`. The current paper already has
  Andrew Zigler as the author, so de-anonymization is already done;
  verify nothing else slips through.

### Change 10 — Page limit verification
- **What:** Verify camera-ready page limit for the workshop (likely 5
  pages; some workshops grant +1 for camera-ready). Default to 5
  pages including references; if +1 is granted, use it for the new
  tables/figures and acronym expansion. If exceeding, trim
  iteratively.
- **Why:** Brief required.
- **How:** Read `latexmk -pdf` output; trim if over. The owner can
  confirm the camera-ready page limit from the camera-ready
  instructions email when it arrives.

## 4. Formal Specification

### Deliverable 1: Revised paper
- `paper/main.tex` — `\documentclass[sigconf]{acmart}` (no review)
- `paper/sections/introduction.tex` — MEP acronym introduced; novelty
  framing paragraph (or in related-work)
- `paper/sections/related-work.tex` — prompt engineering background;
  novelty paragraph distinguishing MEP from spec-driven /
  prompt-engineering / iterative
- `paper/sections/methodology.tex` — MEP acronym substitutions
- `paper/sections/case-study.tex` — Table 2 (12-team summary), Figure
  2 (per-bead completion times), Figure 3 OR augmented Table 1
  (deployment timeline), team-variation subsection
- `paper/sections/context-fluency.tex` — MEP acronym substitutions
- `paper/sections/research-agenda.tex` — honest limitations rewrite,
  expanded future work, Artifact Availability subsection
- `paper/references.bib` — ≥3 prompt engineering entries, possibly +1–2
  for novelty framing comparison literature
- `paper/figures/` — populated with new figure source if not inline TikZ
- `paper/main.pdf` — final compiled PDF, ≤6 pages, ICPS-compatible

### Deliverable 2: Research artifact bundle
- `artifact/README.md` — description, license, Zenodo upload steps,
  inventory
- `artifact/data/` — anonymized hackathon timing/commit/bead data
- `artifact/prompts/` — hackathon prompts + agent configs
- `artifact/agent-harness/` — CLAUDE.md, beads schema, scaffolds used
- `artifact/LICENSE-CC-BY-4.0`, `artifact/LICENSE-MIT`

### Deliverable 3: Pull request
- Branch `camera-ready` → `main`
- Title: "Camera-ready revisions for VibeX 2026 (accepted)"
- Body covering all 10 changes, lists reviewer concerns addressed,
  attaches summary of artifact bundle, includes final PDF metadata
  (page count, build).

## 5. Test Cases (≥10)

TEST: mep-acronym-first-use
INPUT:    grep -n "mise en place (MEP)" paper/sections/*.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 hit, in introduction or methodology (whichever comes first)
RATIONALE: Acronym must be introduced on first use per Reviewer 2.

TEST: mep-acronym-usage
INPUT:    grep -c "MEP" paper/sections/*.tex
EXPECTED: ≥10 occurrences across the paper
RATIONALE: Acronym should be USED throughout, not just introduced.

TEST: prompt-engineering-cited
INPUT:    grep -i "prompt engineering" paper/sections/related-work.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 hit; references.bib has ≥3 prompt-engineering entries
RATIONALE: Reviewer 2 missing-context note.

TEST: novelty-framing-paragraph
INPUT:    grep -E "(spec-driven|iterative|prompt engineering)" paper/sections/related-work.tex paper/sections/introduction.tex
EXPECTED: ≥3 hits across the comparison terms
RATIONALE: Reviewer 3 explicitly asked for distinguishing framing.

TEST: 12-team-table
INPUT:    grep -E "Table.*[Tt]eam" paper/sections/case-study.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 reference to a 12-team artifact summary table
RATIONALE: Reviewer 2 explicitly asked.

TEST: per-bead-figure
INPUT:    grep -E "Figure 2|completion time" paper/sections/case-study.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 hit
RATIONALE: Reviewer 2 asked for results visualizations.

TEST: team-variation-discussion
INPUT:    grep -i "across.*teams\|team.*variation\|other.*teams" paper/sections/case-study.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 hit
RATIONALE: Reviewer 2 specifically asked.

TEST: artifact-availability-section
INPUT:    grep -E "Artifact Availability|artifact.*available" paper/sections/research-agenda.tex paper/main.tex
EXPECTED: ≥1 hit pointing to Zenodo placeholder
RATIONALE: SIGSOFT Open Science Policy compliance.

TEST: artifact-bundle-exists
INPUT:    ls artifact/README.md artifact/data/ artifact/prompts/
EXPECTED: All exist
RATIONALE: Required deliverable.

TEST: documentclass-no-review
INPUT:    head -5 paper/main.tex | grep "documentclass"
EXPECTED: Match `\documentclass[sigconf]{acmart}` — no `review` option
RATIONALE: Camera-ready format.

TEST: limitations-acknowledges-empirical-gap
INPUT:    grep -iE "single (hackathon|case)|control group|operator expertise" paper/sections/research-agenda.tex
EXPECTED: ≥2 hits across the phrases
RATIONALE: Reviewer 1's exact concern visibly addressed.

TEST: future-work-comparative-study
INPUT:    grep -iE "preparation-first.*iteration|comparative study|multi-team replication|longitudinal" paper/sections/research-agenda.tex
EXPECTED: ≥3 of these 4 phrases present
RATIONALE: Convert Review 1's critique into research roadmap.

TEST: pdf-builds-clean
INPUT:    cd paper && latexmk -pdf main.tex 2>&1 | tail -5
EXPECTED: "Output written on main.pdf" with no fatal errors
RATIONALE: Camera-ready must compile.

TEST: page-count-bound
INPUT:    pdfinfo paper/main.pdf | grep Pages
EXPECTED: ≤6 pages (CFP allowed 5; +1 typical for camera-ready)
RATIONALE: Page limit must be respected.

## 6. Open Questions

OQ-01 (P2): Camera-ready page limit — 5 or 6 pages?
- **Recommendation:** Default to 5 pages (the original CFP limit) and
  use any additional page if granted by camera-ready instructions.
  Brief says "Verify track-specific page limits"; the actual
  camera-ready instructions arrive after the acceptance email. Owner
  will verify when those instructions land.
- **Affects:** Trim discipline during revision.

OQ-02 (P3): "70% rework time" claim — keep, soften, or cite?
- **Recommendation:** Soften to "substantial" or similar in this
  revision. The original REVIEW-NOTES.md flagged this as an anecdotal
  claim; reviewers didn't push on it but a careful camera-ready
  shouldn't keep an unsourced specific number.
- **Affects:** Introduction.

OQ-03 (P3): "Two hours" preparation timing claim — keep or reframe?
- **Recommendation:** Keep as the experienced concentrated-prep
  window (with optional footnote acknowledging earlier exploration).
  REVIEW-NOTES.md flagged this; reviewers did not raise it. Don't
  open a wound that didn't get poked.
- **Affects:** Abstract, case study.

OQ-04 (P2): Where does the novelty-framing paragraph live —
related-work.tex or introduction.tex?
- **Recommendation:** Related-work.tex. The intro is already tight
  and the novelty framing requires comparison detail that fits the
  related-work register. Plant a single sentence in the intro pointing
  forward to the comparison.
- **Affects:** Intro flow.

## 7. Future Considerations

- arXiv version (lighter formatting) — owner generates from the same
  source after camera-ready PDF is approved
- Zenodo upload — owner does this; replaces `[TBD]` placeholder with
  the actual DOI in `paper/sections/research-agenda.tex` (or wherever
  Artifact Availability lives) before final EasyChair upload
- Talk slides — separate deliverable, not in this revision

title: paper: rework case study tone and timeline with bead data
description:
## Context
The case study section has promotional language (CEO name-drop, hackathon victory emphasis) that undermines the academic tone. The implementation timeline relies on git commits, but the first commit is monolithic (76 files). Bead closure timestamps (being extracted to data/bead-timestamps.md by another agent) provide a richer timeline with discrete data points.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/sections/case-study.tex`. DO NOT touch any other .tex files. Read `~/cfp/mise/data/bead-timestamps.md` for the bead closure data (it will be there by the time you start).

### Promotional tone fixes:
1. **Remove CEO name-drop**: Line 15-16, change "A panel of judges that included Nicholas Thompson, CEO of The Atlantic, evaluated final presentations" to something like "A panel of industry judges evaluated final presentations." The CEO name is promotional.
2. **Remove victory bragging**: Line 89, "The judges selected it for first place." Either remove entirely or reframe neutrally: "The project received the top evaluation from the judging panel." Actually, even that might be too much — consider just removing the sentence. The point of the case study is the methodology, not the award.
3. **Soften product listing**: Lines 87-89, the feature list ("teacher dashboards, student research workspaces, article annotation tools, citation validation, and a Socratic AI tutor") reads like marketing. Consider shortening to "a full-stack educational platform with multiple user roles and AI-assisted research tools."
4. **"Architecture was correct on first pass"** (line 92): Soften to "the architecture required no structural refactoring" — same meaning, less self-congratulatory.

### Timeline rework using bead data:
5. **Replace git-commit-centric narrative**: Lines 75-80 emphasize the monolithic first commit. Instead, use bead closure timestamps to tell the story: "During the implementation phase, agents closed N beads in M minutes (median closure time: X min). Bead closures were distributed across [time range], with [evidence of parallel execution]." Read data/bead-timestamps.md for exact numbers.
6. **Update or supplement Table 1**: If bead data provides a richer breakdown, consider adding a "Beads closed" column or replacing the commit-based metrics. The table should emphasize the bead-level granularity over git commits.
7. **Rework ratio**: The current "3 bug-fix commits of 9 total" is weak because git commits don't represent the actual work distribution. If bead data shows bug-type beads vs feature-type beads with timestamps, use that instead: e.g., "Of N total beads, M were bug-fix beads, resolved with a median closure time of X minutes."

### Framing adjustment:
8. **Limitations — broader landscape**: In the limitations subsection, after acknowledging the single case study, add 1-2 sentences noting that while this study lacks a control group, the methodology's emphasis on deliberate preparation stands in marked contrast to the dominant iteration-first patterns documented in practitioner literature — this contrast itself motivates formal comparative study (connecting to RQ1).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CEO name removed
- [ ] Victory sentence removed or neutralized
- [ ] Product feature list shortened
- [ ] "Correct on first pass" softened
- [ ] Implementation narrative uses bead closure timestamps
- [ ] Rework metrics use bead data instead of (or alongside) git commits
- [ ] Limitations acknowledge broader landscape contrast
- [ ] Paper compiles cleanly, 5 pages, zero overflows

title: harden: fix claims in introduction and related work
description:
## Context
Three adversarial reviewers identified weaknesses in the introduction and related work sections. These fixes harden the paper against single-blind peer review.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/sections/introduction.tex`, `paper/sections/related-work.tex`, and `paper/references.bib`. DO NOT touch any other .tex files.

### In introduction.tex:
1. **Soften "70% rework" claim** (line 15-16): Change "consuming 70% or more of total development time" to "consuming substantial development time" — the 70% figure is anecdotal from practitioner blogs, not empirical data. Keep the citations but don't attach a specific number.
2. **Operationalize "alignment"**: Add 1-2 sentences after "alignment between developer intent and agent output" defining it concretely: specification compliance (% of planned features implemented as specified), architectural fidelity (design decisions honored in code), and rework ratio (corrective commits / total commits). This gives reviewers a measurable construct.

### In related-work.tex:
3. **Differentiate from RPI**: Where Horthy's RPI is discussed, add 1 sentence clarifying how mise en place differs: RPI prescribes research-plan-implement but does not formalize the tacit knowledge externalization of Phase 1 or the backward-design sequencing that insists all three phases complete before implementation begins.
4. **Add knowledge management reference**: Add brief mention of Nonaka & Takeuchi's knowledge creation theory (SECI model) as prior work on tacit-to-explicit knowledge conversion, connecting it to Phase 1. This addresses the "thin theoretical grounding" critique.

### In references.bib:
5. **Fix date mismatch**: The entry `developer2025mental` has `year = {2023}` but the cite key says 2025. Change the cite key to `developer2023mental` and update all references in ALL .tex files that cite this key.
6. **Add Nonaka & Takeuchi reference**: Add a BibTeX entry for: Nonaka, I. and Takeuchi, H. (1995) "The Knowledge-Creating Company", Oxford University Press.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "70%" removed from introduction, replaced with "substantial"
- [ ] Alignment operationalized with 1-2 sentences defining metrics
- [ ] RPI differentiation sentence added in related work
- [ ] Nonaka & Takeuchi cited in related work for knowledge creation
- [ ] developer2025mental cite key fixed to developer2023mental everywhere
- [ ] Nonaka bib entry added to references.bib
- [ ] Paper still compiles cleanly with `latexmk -pdf main.tex`

title: artifact: build SIGSOFT Open Science bundle (Change 6)
harden: fix abstract, methodology, and case study
description:
## Context
Reviewers flagged issues in the abstract, methodology section, and case study. These are the highest-impact fixes for hardening the paper.

## Task
Fix issues in `paper/main.tex` (abstract only), `paper/sections/methodology.tex`, and `paper/sections/case-study.tex`. DO NOT touch introduction.tex, related-work.tex, context-fluency.tex, research-agenda.tex, or references.bib.

### In main.tex (abstract):
1. **Replace "near-one-shot implementation"**: The data shows 6 bugs and 52 min of polish after initial deployment — this is NOT near-one-shot. Replace with "rapid parallel implementation" or "compressed implementation cycle". Keep the rest of the abstract intact.
2. **Soften "70%" in abstract**: Change "consuming 70% or more of total development time" to "consuming substantial development time" (matching the introduction fix).

### In methodology.tex:
3. **Clarify Integration Verification**: Figure 1 shows "Integration Verification" but the text never defines this stage. Add 1-2 sentences at the end of Phase 3 or after the figure: "Following agent execution, an integration verification stage validates outputs against the specification. The practitioner reviews agent-produced code, files defects as new task records, and dispatches agents for targeted corrections."
4. **Drop or clarify 5.7:1 ratio**: The 5.7:1 "preparation-to-execution ratio" arithmetic is never explained. The actual data shows ~17hr session / ~3hr coding. If using this ratio, state the calculation explicitly. Better: remove "5.7:1" from methodology and keep it only in case-study.tex where it can be explained with the data. Check if 5.7:1 appears in methodology.tex — if so, remove it (it belongs in case study only).

### In case-study.tex:
5. **Explain 5.7:1 ratio**: Where the ratio appears, add a parenthetical: "approximately 5.7:1 (estimated from the ~17-hour primary session span, of which approximately 3 hours comprised active implementation and deployment)". This makes the arithmetic transparent.
6. **Strengthen Limitations**: Add 2-3 sentences acknowledging: (a) the practitioner's dual background in education and software engineering likely contributed to the quality of contextual grounding, especially the pedagogical domain encoding; (b) whether mise en place produces equivalent results for practitioners without domain expertise is an open question (connecting to RQ3).
7. **Quantify alignment in results**: Add 1 sentence reporting a concrete rework metric: "The rework ratio—corrective commits as a proportion of total commits—was 33% (3 bug-fix commits of 9 total), compared to practitioner-reported norms exceeding 50% in unstructured agentic workflows."

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "Near-one-shot" replaced in abstract
- [ ] "70%" softened in abstract
- [ ] Integration Verification defined in methodology text
- [ ] 5.7:1 ratio explained with transparent arithmetic in case study
- [ ] Practitioner expertise confound added to Limitations
- [ ] Rework ratio metric added to results
- [ ] Paper still compiles cleanly with `latexmk -pdf main.tex`
- [ ] Still exactly 5 pages (check with pdfinfo)

title: writing: draft Intro, Context Fluency, Research Agenda, Conclusion
description:
## Context
Introduction (~400 words), Context Fluency (~400 words), Research Agenda + Conclusion (~400 words). These frame the paper and propose novel constructs.

## Task
Write LaTeX for: Section 1 (alignment problem, vibe coding, mise en place metaphor, contributions list), Section 5 (context fluency as emerging skill, components, distinction from prompt engineering, teaching connection), Section 6 (5 research questions with theoretical justification, concluding invitation).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] paper/sections/introduction.tex (~400 words)
- [ ] paper/sections/context-fluency.tex (~400 words)
- [ ] paper/sections/research-agenda.tex (~400 words)
- [ ] All properly cited with \cite{} commands
